notes: All assertions pass 100% in both configurations — these assertions are non-discriminating for a capable model. Claude already knows git worktree mechanics well enough without the skill.

The real value of the skill is qualitative: structured briefing templates for agents, explicit file-ownership doctrine, PR-as-coordination-channel pattern, draft PR workflow for parallel sessions, and the --force-with-lease / rebase-before-push discipline. These don't show up in binary pass/fail.
With-skill runs are 20s slower and use ~7800 more tokens on average (reading skill + reference files). This is the cost of the skill.

Better discriminating assertions would check for: (1) explicit agent briefing template with working-dir + scope + out-of-scope, (2) rebase-before-push advice, (3) draft PR recommendation for in-progress sessions, (4) post-merge cleanup sequence (worktree remove + prune).
The eval set should be redesigned to test skill-unique knowledge rather than general git knowledge.
